event: King Asa with his family — ancestor of Christ
symbols: family grouping — the continuity of the messianic lineage through generations
seated contemplative figure — the weight of royal and ancestral responsibility
triangular spandrel composition — the architectural framework of salvation history
rich garments — royal lineage of the house of David
description: A Sistine Chapel spandrel fresco showing a group of figures within a triangular architectural space. The central figure wears a red robe and green cloak, seated in a contemplative posture, leaning forward with head bowed. Beside this figure are smaller figures — possibly a woman and child. The spandrel is flanked by painted architectural elements including seated figures in niches (sibyls or prophets) and decorative nude figures (ignudi) above. The fresco shows Michelangelo's characteristic muscular, monumental forms.
theological_description: King Asa appears in the genealogy of Jesus Christ recorded in Matthew 1:7-8, as part of the royal line from David through Solomon. Asa was one of Judah's reforming kings who 'did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, as his father David had done' (1 Kings 15:11). He removed idols, deposed his grandmother Maacah for her Asherah pole, and brought the temple treasures back to God's house. Yet even Asa's faithfulness was imperfect — in his later years he relied on foreign alliances rather than the Lord (2 Chronicles 16:7-9) and imprisoned the prophet who rebuked him. Michelangelo includes Asa among Christ's ancestors in the Sistine Chapel to illustrate the theological truth that the Messiah's lineage runs through flawed, complex human beings — sinners in need of the very Savior they would eventually produce. This is the scandal of the Incarnation: God enters human history not through a line of perfect saints but through the messy, faithful-and-failing generations of Israel, demonstrating that 'where sin increased, grace abounded all the more' (Romans 5:20).

scripture_refs.reason: Asa named in the genealogy of Jesus Christ — the direct subject of this spandrel
Asa doing what was right in the Lord's eyes — his positive legacy as a reforming king
Asa's later failure to rely on the Lord — the complexity of his faith
Grace abounding where sin increased — God's messianic line running through imperfect ancestors
